UNICEF Global Health Report

Author

Your Name

Code 
import pandas as pd

# Load the data files into pandas DataFrames
ind1 = pd.read_csv('unicef_indicator_1.csv')
ind2 = pd.read_csv('unicef_indicator_2.csv')
meta = pd.read_csv('unicef_metadata.csv')

# Show the first 5 rows of the metadata to confirm it worked
meta.head()
country alpha_2_code alpha_3_code numeric_code year Population, total GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$) GNI (current US$) Inflation, consumer prices (annual %) Life expectancy at birth, total (years) Military expenditure (% of GDP) Fossil fuel energy consumption (% of total) GDP growth (annual %) Birth rate, crude (per 1,000 people) Hospital beds (per 1,000 people)

Introduction to Global Health

Child mortality and nutritional deficiencies remain critical global issues. This report examines the relationship between Vitamin A supplementation and mortality rates.

Key areas of focus include: * Global distribution of health indicators * The impact of GDP on life expectancy * Trends over recent decades

Code 
from plotnine import *
import geopandas as gpd

# 1. Load the world map directly from the web URL
url = "https://naciscdn.org/naturalearth/110m/cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
world = gpd.read_file(url)

# 2. Merge the world map with your first indicator dataset
# Natural Earth uses 'ISO_A3' for country codes
map_data = world.merge(ind1, left_on='ISO_A3', right_on='alpha_3_code', how='left')

# 3. Draw the map using plotnine
world_map = (
    ggplot(map_data)
    + geom_map(aes(fill='obs_value'))
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(12, 6))
    + labs(title="Global View of Indicator Values", fill="Indicator Value")
)

# Display the map
display(world_map)

Comparing Top Countries

To understand the scale of the issue, let’s look at the top 10 countries with the highest reported values.

Code 
# 1. Filter the data to get the top 10 highest values
top10 = ind1.nlargest(10, 'obs_value')

# 2. Draw the bar chart
bar_chart = (
    ggplot(top10, aes(x='reorder(country, obs_value)', y='obs_value', fill='country'))
    + geom_col()
    + coord_flip() # Flips it horizontally so country names are easy to read
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(legend_position='none')
    + labs(title="Top 10 Countries", x="Country", y="Value")
)

display(bar_chart)

Exploring Correlations: The Economic Factor

A key question in global health is: Does national wealth directly correlate with better health outcomes? To investigate this, we compared our primary health indicator against a secondary metric. The scatterplot below includes a regression line to help visualize the underlying trend: - Points clustered tightly around the line indicate a strong relationship. - A downward slope generally indicates that as one metric (like wealth) increases, the negative health outcome decreases.

Code 
# 1. Merge indicator 1 and 2 to compare them side-by-side
scatter_data = pd.merge(ind1, ind2, on=['country', 'time_period'])

# 2. Draw the scatterplot with the regression line
scatter_plot = (
    ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x='obs_value_x', y='obs_value_y'))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.5, color="#2ca02c")
    + geom_smooth(method='lm', color="red", se=False) # 'lm' creates the regression line
    + theme_light()
    + labs(title="Correlation between Indicators", x="Indicator 1 Value", y="Indicator 2 Value")
)

display(scatter_plot)

Historical Context: Trends Over Time

Understanding the present requires looking at the past. Have global initiatives successfully improved the situation over the last few decades?

The final visualization tracks the historical progression of the indicator across various countries, revealing: 1. Long-term success stories where crisis rates have consistently dropped. 2. Regions where progress has unfortunately stagnated or reversed due to conflict or economic instability.

Code 
# Draw the time-series line chart
time_series = (
    ggplot(ind1, aes(x='time_period', y='obs_value', group='country', color='country'))
    + geom_line(alpha=0.4)
    + theme_classic()
    + theme(legend_position='none')
    + labs(title="Indicator Trend Over Time", x="Year", y="Value")
)

display(time_series)
